title: The Whip Count
premise: You are a <def term="Provincial Delegate">Provincial Delegate</def> in a divided legislature, trying to pass a contested <def term="infrastructure bill">infrastructure bill</def>. You need 60 votes. You have this window before the <def term="committee">committee</def> reports out. Three things move: your <def term="Coalition">Coalition</def> (do you have the votes?), your <def term="Credibility">Credibility</def> (are you seen as honest?), and your <def term="Leverage">Leverage</def> (what can you trade?).
note: This is a disposable test scenario for building the harness. Not canon.

glossary.Provincial Delegate: An elected member of the legislature representing a province.
glossary.infrastructure bill: A proposed law funding roads, bridges, and public works.
glossary.committee: A small group of legislators who review a bill before the full chamber votes.
glossary.Coalition: The bloc of votes you've assembled. You need 60 to pass.
glossary.Credibility: How honest and consistent other legislators believe you are.
glossary.Leverage: What you have to offer or trade in negotiations.

scenes.title: The Whip Count
scenes.body: Your bill needs 60 votes. This morning: 41 committed, 12 hard no, 47 undecided. The undecided cluster around three blocs — the fiscal hawks, the rural delegates, and the reformers who like the bill but distrust you. Committee reports in four days.

buttons.label: Offer the hawks a sunset clause on the budget line
Lock the rural delegates first, build outward
Ask the reformers' leader what it would take

s1_hawks.text: You go straight at the fiscal hawks with a five-year sunset clause. It's a clean, legible offer — and it reads as buying votes. The hawks listen, but the reformers notice you led with a deal.
s1_hawks.desc: Go straight at the fiscal hawks with a direct concession (a sunset clause). WHO: hawks. WHAT: flat concession. HOW: direct offer.

s1_rural.text: You lock the rural delegates first — they want the roads most. It builds a solid base, but the hawks see you consolidating the easy votes and dig in.
s1_rural.desc: Lock the rural delegates first and build outward. WHO: rural bloc. WHAT: consolidate base. HOW: sequential coalition-building.

s1_reformers.text: You call the reformers' leader and ask, plainly, what it would take. Asking rather than offering reads as serious. They respect it — and now you owe them a real answer.
s1_reformers.desc: Approach the reformers' leader and ASK what it would take, rather than offering. WHO: reformers. WHAT: open the ask. HOW: private inquiry.

s1_bridge.text: You look for a bridge — someone trusted in two camps at once. You find a hawk with rural ties and ask what SHAPE the bill would need to take for someone of her reputation to carry it. That's a stronger opening than any single offer: you've found the path to the votes, not just asked for them.
s1_bridge.desc: Find a BRIDGE ACTOR — someone trusted in two blocs at once (e.g. a hawk with rural ties) — and ask what shape the bill needs so they'll carry it to both camps as their own idea. WHO: an intermediary spanning blocs. WHAT: conditional reshaping. HOW: advocacy-by-proxy. This is a move the buttons do not offer.

outOfBounds.text: That's a real lever, but not one available in this committee fight — the question in front of you is how to move the undecided blocs before the report.

scenes.title: The Price
scenes.body: The path forward runs through reshaping the bill's funding mechanism. But reopening it in committee means the reformers — who already distrust you — will read a mid-stream rewrite as a backroom deal. You have four days.

buttons.label: Make the change openly, own it as a strengthening amendment
Let an ally introduce it, stay in the background

s2_open.text: You make the change openly and own it. Transparency costs you nothing with the reformers and signals confidence. Some hawks grumble at the exposure.
s2_open.desc: Make the funding change openly in committee and own it as a strengthening amendment. HOW: fully public/transparent.

s2_proxy.text: You let an ally carry it while you stay back. It moves, but the reformers notice your fingerprints anyway, and the indirection reads as exactly the maneuvering they suspected.
s2_proxy.desc: Have an ally introduce the change while you stay in the background. HOW: indirect/proxy.

s2_preempt.text: You brief the reformers privately before the amendment surfaces. Pre-empting their suspicion is the move that converts them — they'd rather be told than discover.
s2_preempt.desc: Brief the reformers privately BEFORE the amendment surfaces, to pre-empt their suspicion. WHO: reformers. HOW: private pre-emption.

s2_reframe.text: You don't just make the change — you reframe it. You sell the sunset itself as the feature: the taper guarantees the reformers a future negotiation from strength, with a predictable off-ramp instead of a cliff. You turn the bill's biggest vulnerability into its pitch. The room goes quiet in the way that means people are doing the arithmetic instead of arguing.
s2_reframe.desc: Reframe the bill's weakness (that it sunsets) as its selling point — the sunset guarantees the skeptics future leverage and a predictable off-ramp. WHAT: reframe-as-mutual-benefit. A persuasion move the buttons do not offer.

outOfBounds.text: An interesting thought, but the bill's funding mechanism is the lever in play right now — the question is how you reshape it without losing the reformers.

scenes.title: The Last Votes
scenes.body: You're close. Two votes short of 60. The remaining gettable votes are the three delegates who like the bill least — a hard fiscal hawk, a rural delegate who feels betrayed you rewrote 'her' bill, and the reformer leader who respects you now but hasn't decided if respect is a vote. One real play left.

buttons.label: Go back to the hawk with numbers
Ask the reformer straight for their vote

s3_hawk.text: You bring the hawk hard numbers and shave the cost. It works, barely — but reopening the math rattles the coalition you'd built.
s3_hawk.desc: Go back to the hard fiscal hawk with numbers, shave the cost. WHO: hawk. WHAT: cost concession.

s3_rural.text: You repair the rural delegate with a genuine apology, not a tactical one. She comes back, and comes back as an advocate — a wound turned into an ally.
s3_rural.desc: Repair the betrayed rural delegate with a genuine (not tactical) apology. WHO: rural delegate. WHAT: relationship repair.

s3_reformer.text: You ask the reformer straight. You've earned the right to. He gives you his personal yes and pulls one waverer with him.
s3_reformer.desc: Ask the reformer leader straight for their personal vote. WHO: reformer. WHAT: direct ask.

s3_convene.text: You put the two hardest votes in a room together and let them solve each other's problem — the hawk wants it cheaper, the rural delegate wants it built. They find the savings themselves, and walk out co-owners. You never had to spend a thing; you manufactured the votes.
s3_convene.desc: CONVENE the two hardest, opposed votes (hawk + rural) in one room and let THEM solve each other's problem — the hawk wants it cheaper, the rural delegate wants it built — so they co-author the savings and walk out co-owners. WHO: two adversaries together. WHAT: facilitate-mutual-solution. HOW: brokered room. A move the buttons do not offer.

outOfBounds.text: A real lever in general — but with one day and the votes this close, the play in front of you is these three delegates.

ending.title: The Vote
ending.text: The bill reports out and passes. How it passed — and what you became on the way to 60 — is written in your three meters. A coalition built on trust holds; one bought with leverage frays; one that cost you your credibility passes but doesn't last. You reached one of several endings the structure allows. A different sequence of choices lands somewhere else entirely — that's the point.
